overlay_source.policy: Prefer semantic overlays for section-local reader-only edits; graduate to curated manuscript files only when the edit is too broad for overlays.

graduation_criteria: Generated reader source plus overlays are reviewed and found insufficient for final human-reader prose.

Every curated chapter records its generated-reader baseline, live-source commit or tag, and reconciliation status.

The release record names curated reader source only after review actually happens.

reader_handoff_contract.single_thesis: Advanced AI should be built as a governed stack of bounded capabilities, evidence states, executable contracts, resource-aware routes, and reviewable artifacts, so self-improvement can proceed only through visible claims, receipts, residuals, and rollback paths.

part_arcs.title: Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance
part_arcs.reader_arc: The reader first learns why advanced intelligence needs boundaries, evidence discipline, contestable values, stable capability identity, security, and recursive-improvement limits before any impressive capability can be trusted.
part_arcs.reader_stakes: Without this part, the book would look like an engineering catalog rather than an answer to authority, failure, value conflict, and self-improvement risk.
part_arcs.handoff_payoff: By the end of the part, the reader should treat alignment as a governed control architecture rather than a promise attached to a model.

part_arcs.title: Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution
part_arcs.reader_arc: The reader then follows an intent as it becomes executable work, planned steps, semantic representations, accountable context, verification records, belief updates, typed jobs, tool effects, and reusable procedures.
part_arcs.reader_stakes: Without this part, the stack would have principles but no accountable path from a human request to a completed artifact.
part_arcs.handoff_payoff: By the end of the part, the reader should see cognition as a chain of contracts whose failures remain inspectable instead of disappearing into chat output.

part_arcs.title: Part III - Routing, Compression, Representation, and Substrates
part_arcs.reader_arc: The third part explains how a governed stack chooses specialists, readiness states, compute locations, compression modes, speed paths, budgets, mathematical substrates, and cyclic memory structures without confusing efficiency with evidence.
part_arcs.reader_stakes: Without this part, the book would leave efficiency as a vague hope instead of a governed allocation problem with residuals and negative controls.
part_arcs.handoff_payoff: By the end of the part, the reader should understand that efficient ASI is a routing and representation discipline, not merely faster token production.

part_arcs.title: Part IV - Evidence, Implementation, and the Living Book
part_arcs.reader_arc: The final part turns the architecture back onto its own claims through proofs, benchmarks, governed feedback, stewardship, integration traces, implementation references, prototype sequence, publication method, and open research debt.
part_arcs.reader_stakes: Without this part, the book would ask for belief instead of showing how claims are tested, limited, corrected, and carried forward.
part_arcs.handoff_payoff: By the end of the part, the reader should know which pieces are argued, which are mechanized or measured, and what work remains before stronger claims are honest.

signature_ideas.label: Stack, Not Model
signature_ideas.reader_promise: Capability becomes safer and more useful when the reader can name the layer, boundary, receipt, and accountable owner of each action.

signature_ideas.label: Authority Before Action
signature_ideas.reader_promise: No component should act, delegate, expose data, or spend resources without an inspectable authority record and refusal route.

signature_ideas.label: Evidence States
signature_ideas.reader_promise: The book stays honest by separating what is imagined, argued, source-derived, tested, measured, mechanized, deprecated, or refuted.

signature_ideas.label: Residual Honesty
signature_ideas.reader_promise: Unresolved risk, verification debt, displaced cost, and uncertainty should remain visible rather than being hidden behind a successful output.

signature_ideas.label: Typed Work
signature_ideas.reader_promise: Intelligent work becomes governable when requests, jobs, effects, outputs, approvals, and audit events are typed artifacts rather than informal impressions.

signature_ideas.label: Accountable Memory
signature_ideas.reader_promise: Context is not just more tokens; it is state with provenance, scope, taint, deletion, adequacy, and downstream accountability.

signature_ideas.label: Routing as Governance
signature_ideas.reader_promise: Choosing a model, specialist, compute host, or method is itself an authority and evidence decision with costs and residuals.

signature_ideas.label: Transactional Improvement
signature_ideas.reader_promise: Self-improvement should look like controlled replacement, qualification, monitoring, rollback, and residual ownership rather than unchecked mutation.

signature_ideas.label: Proofs as Receipts
signature_ideas.reader_promise: Formal work should function as scoped receipts attached to claims, contracts, and replayable artifacts instead of decorative rigor.

signature_ideas.label: Living Book
signature_ideas.reader_promise: The book itself should behave like the system it describes: versioned, evidence-aware, reviewer-facing, and capable of honest correction.
